approach.reasoning: The agent largely framed the problem correctly and used appropriate data sources and tools: they searched for EIA utility identifiers for Xcel subsidiaries and invoked the OpenEI tariffs API (get_utility_tariffs) scoped by EIA ID and by state, which is the right dataset to retrieve commercial service classes and capacity/demand charges. However there are notable gaps in the execution. The trace shows no evidence the agent applied the critical filter for customer classes with demand >500 kW, nor that they identified or compared the service classes with the maximum demand (capacity) charges across subsidiaries — the suggested analytical step of filtering and then extracting maximum demand charges is missing. Some web searches appear redundant and a few EIA ID lookups may be incomplete or imprecise, increasing risk of missed subsidiaries. Overall the tool choices and high-level workflow were appropriate, but the agent failed to complete essential filtering, aggregation and comparison steps required to answer the question fully.

accuracy.reasoning: No agent answer content was provided to evaluate. Therefore none of the expected attributes could be found or matched. If an agent response is supplied, re-evaluate each attribute against the expected values with the specified numeric tolerances.

sources.reasoning: No explicit sources were provided by the agent. The task specifically requires using OpenEI tariffs data and EIA utility identifiers (or equivalent authoritative market/utility portals) to identify commercial service classes and capacity/demand charges for Xcel Energy subsidiaries by state. Reasonable authoritative sources that should have been cited include: the OpenEI Tariff Database (tariff IDs or URLs for relevant Xcel tariffs), EIA utility ID pages for Xcel subsidiaries (to confirm service territories), Xcel Energy tariff schedules or state PUC tariff repositories for Colorado, Minnesota, Wisconsin, Texas (and other states served by Xcel subsidiaries), and any OpenEI API/query results used. Because none of these or equivalent authoritative sources were named, the claim cannot be validated and the source validity is very low.

attribute_details.name: States served by Xcel (in dataset)
attribute_details.expected: Texas, Wisconsin, Michigan, Colorado, Minnesota, North Dakota, South Dakota

attribute_details.name: Service class with highest applicable peak-demand charge by state/region
attribute_details.expected: Texas: Secondary General Service; Wisconsin/Michigan: Cg-9.1 Large General Time-of-Day Secondary Mandatory; Colorado: Secondary Standby Service (SST); Minnesota/North Dakota/South Dakota: Multiple General Service classes (largest applicable general service)

attribute_details.name: Approximate demand charge ($/kW) by state/region
attribute_details.expected: Texas: ≈ $23/kW; Wisconsin/Michigan: ≈ $17/kW; Colorado: ≈ $20/kW; Minnesota/North Dakota/South Dakota: ≈ $17/kW

attribute_details.name: Applicability / mandatory thresholds for large commercial customers
attribute_details.expected: Texas: applies to >25 kW secondary customers (but excludes resale/shared service and oil/natural gas production); Wisconsin/Michigan: mandatory for customers with 15-min demand ≥200 kW for 4 of 12 months; other regions: multiple riders and thresholds, customer-specific enrollment rules

attribute_details.name: Overall comparative conclusion
attribute_details.expected: Texas shows the highest peak demand charge among the regions in the OpenEI tariff data
